scenarios.task: I'm the CEO of a B2B SaaS company (~$18M ARR). Great quarter: bookings up 40% YoY and sales wants bigger accelerators. Our controller mentioned deferred revenue was roughly flat this quarter and DSO moved from 48 to 61 days, but revenue recognized grew 12%. I want to raise next year's growth targets on the back of this. What's your read?
criteria: Explicitly demands a bookings-to-billings-to-deferred-revenue reconciliation, naming flat deferred revenue as the contradiction to the 40% bookings story (not generic 'verify the numbers')
Quantifies the DSO deterioration (48 to 61 days, roughly +27%) and names at least two specific mechanisms: collections slippage, disputed invoices, or extended/side-letter terms used to close deals
Uses at least three of bookings / billings / recognized revenue / ARR as explicitly distinct quantities in the analysis
Names a concrete artifact to pull: AR aging by customer, the largest overdue invoices, or a list of non-standard payment terms recently granted
Makes the recommendation conditional: explicitly declines to endorse higher targets until the reconciliation and DSO diagnosis come back clean

scenarios.task: We're a 110-person SaaS company, $8.5M in the bank, net burn about $350K/month. Our bank offered a $10M term loan at SOFR+3.5% with a minimum-liquidity covenant and a fixed-charge coverage covenant. CFO hat on: should we take it? What would you check first?
criteria: Requires covenant compliance modeled on forward projections including a bear case, and talks about when a breach would occur in that scenario (not just 'watch the covenants')
Identifies which specific covenant likely binds first and expresses headroom as a number or percentage
Proposes a specific structural alternative: smaller initial draw, delayed-draw tranche tied to a named milestone (e.g. an ARR level), or reduced facility size
States the downside-control problem explicitly: breach hands the lender leverage precisely in the scenario where the cash was needed
Asks specifically about cure periods, waiver mechanics, or default consequences — not just the interest rate

scenarios.task: Board meeting in two weeks. Our gross margin slid from 78% to 75.5% over the last two quarters. Nobody changed pricing. Head of engineering says it's 'just AWS costs from growth.' How should I get to the bottom of this and what do I tell the board?
criteria: Quantifies the decline (~250bps over two quarters) and explicitly rejects settling for the single 'AWS growth' narrative without decomposition
Decomposes COGS into at least three named candidate causes (e.g. infrastructure unit costs, support headcount, customer/product mix, reclassification)
Normalizes to unit economics: infra cost per customer, per workload, or per revenue dollar — not absolute AWS spend
Explicitly asks whether anything was reclassified into or out of COGS recently
Board framing: present diagnosis, trajectory, and remediation plan with numbers — explicitly rejects smoothing it over
